source.description: Six-panel contact sheet generated with the process-first workflow, then cropped into step frames and the finished image.
source.notes: Chosen after a quick event check because Independence Day is approaching in early July 2026.

process_strategy: A six-panel process builds a small porch flag from pole and wave guides to stripes, star field, porch rail, and restrained red and blue pencil accents before the final keeper-line pass.

final_elements.name: angled flag pole
final_elements.description: A short pole leaning slightly upward from a simple porch mount.

final_elements.name: waving flag rectangle
final_elements.description: A soft wavy rectangular flag attached to the pole.

final_elements.name: stripe bands
final_elements.description: Several curved horizontal bands following the flag wave.

final_elements.name: star field block
final_elements.description: A small blue canton block in the upper pole-side corner.

final_elements.name: simple porch rail
final_elements.description: A few straight rail boards behind and below the small flag.

final_elements.name: small star dots
final_elements.description: Tiny star-like dots inside the flag field, kept simple enough to draw.

final_elements.name: red and blue shading
final_elements.description: Restrained red and blue colored-pencil accents on the stripes and star field.

frames.visible_job: Places the angled pole, small mount, and porch rail guide.
Adds the wavy flag rectangle attached to the pole.
Adds curved stripe bands and the star field block.
Adds the porch rail boards and tiny star dots inside the field.
Adds restrained red and blue colored-pencil shading to the existing flag parts.

final_step.visible_job: Only strengthens the pole, flag edge, stripes, field, rail, star dots, and existing color accents.

rejection_checks: No new flag part, rail board, star field, stripe system, or color choice should first appear in the final image.
The flag must keep the same pole angle, wave shape, scale, and porch placement in every frame.
The finish must stay a casual sketch, not a polished poster or exact flag diagram.
